extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam box, a heatbed for heating content, a fan for temperature distribution, two internal temperature sensors, one external temperature sensor, a Raspberry Pi controller, a PCB, and a power supply. It regulates temperature within the box regardless of internal content, with operational focus on thermal inertia modeling and real-time monitoring. Key characteristics include physical-thermal dynamics, sensor-based feedback control, and integration with a Digital Twin for predictive analysis.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Digital Twin services include: 1) Real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboard using time-series data from controller and sensor messages; 2) On-demand calibration using automated calibration processes (section 3.3) for parameter estimation (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K for Model A); 3) Anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation via Kalman filter using Model B (C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K, C_heater=33.65 J/K, G_heater=0.87 J/K); 4) What-if simulations through co-simulations with historical/real-time PT data; 5) Self-adaptation loop for dynamic parameter adjustment when new objects (e.g., cold water) are introduced.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tools/technologies include: 1) Raspberry Pi as controller for actuation and data acquisition; 2) Open-source hardware/software repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) for calibration and simulation; 3) Simulation models (Model A/B) with calibrated parameters for thermal inertia prediction; 4) Kalman filter for state estimation; 5) Time-series database for dashboard visualization; 6) Sensor network with voltage-controlled fan (12V/18V experiments) and temperature sensors (T2/T3 positioning); 7) PCB for electrical component integration; 8) Power supply system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time visualization of incubator status is achieved through continuous data relay to a time series database, with calibration processes executed on-demand. Kalman filters for anomaly detection operate in real-time, correlating measurement data to non-measured heatbed temperature. Co-simulations use historical or real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT) with temporal granularity tied to sensor sampling rates (e.g., temperature data uploaded periodically to RabbitMQ server).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Two distinct models (Model A and Model B) are developed with different parameter sets (2 vs. 4 free parameters). The system employs a centralized architecture via RabbitMQ for message passing between components (controller, low-level driver, and DT). Coordination mechanisms include automated calibration processes and statechart-based controller logic, with responsibilities divided between model calibration, state estimation, and anomaly detection.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin integrates modeling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization through calibration algorithms that update CPS and environment models with parameter estimates. Components are orchestrated via RabbitMQ for bidirectional communication, with the controller's statechart managing actuation delays. System-wide coordination includes Kalman filter-based heatbed state estimation and self-adaptation loops triggered by new objects placed in the incubator.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Communication with external systems occurs via RabbitMQ, an open-source message broker, enabling data exchange between the Physical Twin and Digital Twin. The system relies on RabbitMQ's publish-subscribe model for real-time data streaming, but no explicit mention of interoperability standards or integration with enterprise systems is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations for dynamics), analytical (heat equations like Q = cmΔT), and calibration models. Data sources are sensors on the Physical Twin (PT) and historical data. Algorithms include fault detectors, Kalman filters for state estimation, and optimizers. Models are calibrated using PT data, with Model B (four parameters) showing better thermal inertia prediction. Co-simulations use real-time/historical PT data for what-if scenarios. Models are integrated with monitoring and optimization algorithms for anomaly detection and self-adaptation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include a heatbed for temperature control and a fan for uniform air distribution. Control mechanisms involve a controller with statechart logic (Figure 8) and software systems for communication. Actuation ranges are governed by heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C) parameters. Safety constraints include temperature limits (LL/UL). Remote control interfaces are via software systems and drivers for real-time adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors measure temperature inside the styrofoam box, with spatial distribution ensured by the fan. Measurement capabilities include temperature tracking, with sampling frequencies and data transmission rates inferred from calibration processes. Accuracy is validated via Model B's thermal inertia predictions. Data collection mechanisms involve continuous sensor networks and time-series databases for real-time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires calibration algorithms to update parameters with PT data. Validation uses Kalman filters for heatbed state estimation and co-simulations for what-if scenarios. Uncertainty sources include assumptions (e.g., uniform heat distribution, constant specific heat capacity). Error detection relies on anomaly detection algorithms and model B's superior inertia prediction. Quality assurance involves iterative calibration, model B's four-parameter accuracy, and real-time data correlation with non-measured heatbed temperatures.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from Physical Twin (PT) include temperature measurements collected via sensors (e.g., styrofoam box temperature, heatbed temperature) transmitted through RabbitMQ server. Data types: temperature readings (°C), control states (fan/heatbed status). Communication protocol: RabbitMQ with AMQP protocol. Transmission frequency: periodic readings (Document 3: 'low-level driver periodically reads temperature data'), event-triggered updates (e.g., temperature threshold breaches). Event triggers: temperature deviations, system state changes. Data quality assurance: calibration algorithms (Document 1), preprocessing via RabbitMQ. Data integration: RabbitMQ relays data to Digital Twin for simulation and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flows include commands to regulate fan/heatbed (e.g., 'turn on heatbed', 'adjust fan speed') sent via RabbitMQ. Command types: actuation signals (binary on/off for bang-bang control). Message format: JSON-based commands with temperature setpoints. Validation: safe protocol in low-level driver (Document 3). Feedback: temperature data uploaded to communication server for state verification. Conflict resolution: prioritization of safety protocols over control commands. Command confirmation: state updates relayed back to Digital Twin via RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocols: RabbitMQ (AMQP), MQTT (implied via message brokering). Network topology: edge (Raspberry Pi) + cloud (RabbitMQ server). Bandwidth: lightweight messaging (RabbitMQ's 'mature, lightweight' profile). Latency: low for real-time control (Document 3: 'controller waits after actuation'). Reliability: message persistence in RabbitMQ, redundancy via distributed architecture. Security: optocouplers for electrical isolation (Document 6), TLS/SSL (implied by RabbitMQ's secure deployment).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platforms: edge (Raspberry Pi), cloud (RabbitMQ server). Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi (ARM Cortex-A53), RabbitMQ server (varies). Storage: time series database for telemetry data. Scalability: modular architecture (separate controller, driver, communication server). Deployment models: edge-cloud hybrid. Availability: redundant RabbitMQ nodes, failover mechanisms (implied by 'mature' RabbitMQ).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process involves developing dynamic models (Model A and B) with calibration methods, integrating data from physical twin (PT) sensors, and using algorithms like Kalman filters for state estimation. The DT evolves through iterative calibration, model refinement, and co-simulation with PT data. Version control and evolution strategies are implied via open-source repository [3] and iterative model development (e.g., Model B with four parameters outperforms Model A with two parameters).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides real-time visualization via dashboard integration, anomaly detection using Kalman filters on Model B, what-if simulations via co-simulations with historical/real-time PT data, and self-adaptation loops to adjust controller parameters (e.g., heating duration H, waiting duration C). Decision-making leverages analytics from model calibration, fault detection, and state estimation to optimize PT operations.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9


**Assumption 1** _The temperature inside the box is distributed uniformly. This was ac-_
_complished by deploying a fan inside the box ensuring distribution of the air inside the_
_box. We test this assumption in section 4._


**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_
_generated by the fan was ignored as well._


Two different models were built:


**Model A:** relies on all assumptions.


**Model B:** relies on all assumptions except Assumption 4.


To obtain the dynamic models of the temperature, the model was developed by
considering the way the energy flows. If the amount of the energy used for heating the
air is acquired, then this energy can be converted into the temperature inside the box
by calculating a basic heat equation:

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided

reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.

a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_

air is acquired, then this energy can be converted into the temperature inside the box
by calculating a basic heat equation:

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1
block_4:
the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

9

_box. We test this assumption in section 4._


**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_
_generated by the fan was ignored as well._


Two different models were built:


**Model A:** relies on all assumptions.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
